questions.question: Which country was the first to use paper money?
questions.explanation: China invented paper money during the Tang Dynasty (618–907 AD) and it became widespread during the Song Dynasty. The Mongol rulers further developed the system. Marco Polo was astonished by paper money during his travels to China.
questions.memory_hook: China: they invented paper, then figured out you could print money on it.

questions.question: The Cuban Missile Crisis occurred during the presidency of which U.S. leader?
questions.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis occurred in October 1962 during President John F. Kennedy's administration. The 13-day standoff between the U.S. and Soviet Union, after Soviet missiles were discovered in Cuba, is considered the closest the Cold War came to nuclear conflict.
questions.memory_hook: Kennedy vs. Khrushchev — K vs. K, 13 days, almost the end.

questions.question: What sport uses a 'puck'?
questions.explanation: A puck is the small, hard rubber disc used in ice hockey. It is 1 inch thick, 3 inches in diameter, and weighs between 5.5 and 6 ounces. Pucks are frozen before games to reduce bouncing on the ice.
questions.memory_hook: Puck — it rhymes with duck, and it slides like one on ice.

questions.question: How many points is a touchdown worth in American football?
questions.explanation: A touchdown is worth 6 points in American football. After scoring a touchdown, teams can attempt a point-after-touchdown kick (1 point) or a two-point conversion by running or passing into the end zone again.
questions.memory_hook: 6 for touchdown — then add 1 or 2 for the extra point or conversion.

questions.question: Which 1994 Disney film features the song 'Circle of Life'?
questions.explanation: 'Circle of Life' opens The Lion King (1994) and was written by Elton John and Tim Rice. The film features music that went on to inspire a massively successful Broadway musical. It remains one of the highest-grossing animated films of all time.
questions.memory_hook: Circle of Life — Simba rises, the circle continues.

questions.question: Who directed the 'Dark Knight' trilogy?
questions.explanation: Christopher Nolan directed Batman Begins (2005), The Dark Knight (2008), and The Dark Knight Rises (2012). The trilogy is credited with revitalizing the superhero film genre. The Dark Knight received widespread critical acclaim and Heath Ledger won a posthumous Oscar for his Joker portrayal.
questions.memory_hook: Nolan = No lantern. He made Batman darker than ever.

questions.question: Which artist is known as the 'King of Pop'?
questions.explanation: Michael Jackson earned the title 'King of Pop' due to his extraordinary impact on popular music and culture. His 1982 album 'Thriller' remains the best-selling album of all time, and his signature moonwalk dance move became iconic worldwide.
questions.memory_hook: King of Pop — MJ wore the crown the whole time.

questions.question: The fall of Constantinople in 1453 ended which empire?
questions.explanation: The fall of Constantinople on May 29, 1453, to Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II ended the Byzantine Empire — the eastern continuation of the Roman Empire that had survived for nearly 1,000 years after Rome's fall. It marked the end of the Middle Ages.
questions.memory_hook: Byzantine = East Rome. It lasted until 1453 — almost 1,000 years after Rome fell.

questions.question: Which is the world's longest mountain range?
questions.explanation: The Andes stretches approximately 7,000 kilometers along the western coast of South America, running through Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, Chile, and Argentina. It is the longest above-water mountain range, though the Mid-Atlantic Ridge is longer if you count underwater ranges.
questions.memory_hook: Andes = world's longest mountain range. 7,000 km through 7 countries. The Himalayas are taller, but the Andes are longer — they run almost the entire length of a continent.

questions.question: How long does light from the Sun take to reach Earth?
questions.explanation: Light travels at 299,792 km per second, and the Sun is about 150 million km from Earth. That works out to approximately 8 minutes and 20 seconds. This means the sunlight you see right now actually left the Sun over 8 minutes ago — you're always looking at the Sun's recent past.
questions.memory_hook: Sunlight = 8 minutes old when it hits you. The Sun you see is 8 minutes in the past. If the Sun disappeared right now, you wouldn't know for over 8 minutes.

questions.question: Tierra del Fuego, the island at the southernmost tip of South America, is divided between Argentina and which other country?
questions.explanation: Tierra del Fuego ('Land of Fire') is the archipelago at the very bottom of South America, split between Argentina (east) and Chile (west). The island was named by Ferdinand Magellan in 1520 after he saw fires lit by the indigenous Selknam and Yaghan peoples. The city of Ushuaia on the Argentine side is known as 'the end of the world' and is the southernmost city in the world. The Beagle Channel, where Charles Darwin observed nature during the HMS Beagle voyage, borders it.
questions.memory_hook: Tierra del Fuego = Argentina + Chile, southern tip of S. America. Named by Magellan (1520) for indigenous fires. Ushuaia = 'end of the world,' southernmost city. Darwin's Beagle Channel runs through it.

questions.question: What is the time it takes for half of a radioactive substance to decay called?
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time required for half of the atoms in a radioactive sample to decay into another element. Each radioactive isotope has a unique half-life: carbon-14 has a half-life of about 5,730 years (used in carbon dating for objects up to ~50,000 years old); uranium-238 has a half-life of 4.5 billion years; some synthetic elements have half-lives of milliseconds. After one half-life, 50% remains; after two, 25%; after ten half-lives, less than 0.1% remains.
questions.memory_hook: Half-life = time for half a radioactive sample to decay. Carbon-14: 5,730 years (used to date organic material up to 50,000 years old). Uranium-238: 4.5 billion years. Each radioactive element has a unique, predictable decay rate — nature's atomic clock.
